meta.phase: Two crises at once — Hormuz runs as a controlled high-risk corridor with safe-passage talks underway in Oman, while Russia's diesel export ban opens a second, downstream shock; crude contained (Brent $76 Fri close), European diesel margins at a record

events.headline: Hormuz safe-passage talks resume in Oman — U.S. demands an open-lanes guarantee
events.summary: Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araqchi arrives in Oman for talks with a U.S. team (Vance, Rubio, Witkoff, Kushner). The question has shifted from a simple ceasefire to freedom of navigation: Washington wants Iran to publicly guarantee that all Hormuz lanes stay open, that ships will not be fired on, and that no tolls are imposed. Trump says talks will continue even as he calls the ceasefire over.

sources.label: Al-Monitor — US seeks Iranian pledge to free up Strait of Hormuz
CNN — Iran FM in Oman to discuss Strait of Hormuz (live)

events.headline: Russia bans diesel exports — a second, downstream shock opens
events.summary: Russia bans all diesel exports (through 31 July) after Ukrainian drone strikes cut refinery output. Loadings collapse to about 234,000 b/d over 1–10 July (Kpler), from ~400,000 in June and a 2025 average near 817,000; European diesel margins hit a record $60.17/bbl. Removing Russian barrels forces buyers to compete for Atlantic-basin supply — a middle-distillate squeeze that reaches trucks, ships and farms before it shows in headline crude.

sources.label: Moscow Times — Russia bans diesel exports after drone strikes
SCMP — Russia's diesel export ban deals fresh blow to strained market

events.headline: Hormuz runs as a controlled high-risk corridor, not a normal lane
events.summary: The strait is neither closed nor normal. A handful of tankers cross on the U.S.-protected Omani-side route — two supertankers did so on 9–10 July — while at least eight vessels U-turned over Friday–Saturday and Iran presses traffic toward a northern lane through its own waters; tankers were struck near the Oman coast on 7 July. Passage is possible only under exceptional risk-management arrangements.

sources.label: Bloomberg — Oil tankers still cross Hormuz on Oman side despite flare-up

events.headline: Oil closes the week higher but below panic — Brent $76, WTI $71
events.summary: Brent settles at $76.01 and WTI at $71.41 on Friday, up about 5% and 4% on the week — elevated and acutely headline-sensitive, but still pricing eventual containment rather than a prolonged, total loss of Gulf exports.

sources.label: CNBC — Oil posts weekly gain as Middle East supply risks persist

events.headline: Ceasefire declared over; U.S.–Iran strikes resume
events.summary: President Trump declares the three-week-old interim ceasefire effectively over at the NATO summit in Ankara. U.S. strikes on Iran resume and Iran retaliates against U.S. positions across the Gulf (Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar). A gas carrier catches fire after being struck about eight nautical miles east of Limah in the Strait — the framework holding Gulf passage together is gone.

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Hormuz shipping grinds to halt as US–Iran resume fighting

events.headline: Oil barely flinches — Brent ~6% to near $80, then eases
events.summary: Despite the strikes, crude stays strikingly calm: Brent rises about 6% to a two-week high near $80 on 8 July before easing to about $76 the next session, still in contango — an order of magnitude below February's spike to $126. Lloyd's-market war-risk cover for a Hormuz transit runs 2–6% of hull value (Marsh), up from a fraction of a percent before the war.

sources.label: Al Jazeera — Hormuz shipping grinds to halt as US–Iran resume fighting
Our analysis: The Second Shock Is Not the First

events.headline: Hormuz traffic slows to a near standstill; JMIC raises threat to 'severe'
events.summary: Visible traffic through the Strait slows to a near standstill: Lloyd's List Intelligence cannot identify a single large vessel transiting the U.S.-coordinated Omani corridor with its transponder active since 7 July, though vessels keep crossing dark — the strait is constrained, not closed. The U.S. Navy-led JMIC raises the transit threat to its highest level, 'severe'; U.S. NAVCENT stresses no nation can close or control the strait.

sources.label: gCaptain — Hormuz tanker traffic at near standstill as attacks strain MoU
Lloyd's List — Strait of Hormuz crisis

events.headline: Both sides stand down; talks resume
events.summary: After the 27 June exchange, the U.S. and Iran agree to halt the tit-for-tat strikes and return to talks (reportedly in Qatar), with a commitment to let shipping flow safely through the Strait. The fragile ceasefire holds. Oil keeps falling — Brent ~$72.6, WTI ~$69.8, down 10%+ on the week — as Hormuz oil flows recover to ~4.8M bpd — the highest since the war began, but still only about a third of the ~15M bpd pre-war norm.

events.headline: U.S. strikes Iran after a second tanker is hit
events.summary: An Iranian one-way drone strikes a second tanker — the Panama-flagged M/T Kiku, carrying ~2M barrels of crude — near the Strait. The U.S. answers with airstrikes on Iranian air-defence, surveillance, drone-storage and minelaying sites (CENTCOM). Iran retaliates with ballistic missiles and drones at U.S. bases in Bahrain (the Fifth Fleet) and Kuwait (the Ali Al Salem airbase). Both sides accuse the other of breaking the 60-day ceasefire.

events.headline: Cargo ship struck; Hormuz effectively shut again
events.summary: The Singapore-flagged container ship Ever Lovely is hit by an "unknown projectile" off Oman on the UN-backed route — bridge damage, no casualties. A U.S. official attributes it to an Iranian drone; Iran has not claimed it. The IMO suspends the evacuation of stranded ships, leaving transits effectively halted.

events.headline: Iran reasserts routing control; reopening stalls
events.summary: After a weekend spike toward ~90 transits, Iran's IRGC reasserts routing control over the Strait — only its declared route is permitted, "violators will be dealt with" — and transits collapse back toward ~5 a day, with hundreds of ships still stranded. The U.S. disputes any closure.

events.headline: Brent falls to ~$73, WTI below $70
events.summary: Crude slides for a fourth straight session — Brent to about $73, WTI below $70, its lowest since late February — as the market leans on the 60-day diplomatic framework, the OFAC licence on Iranian barrels and ample Atlantic-basin supply, largely looking through the contested physical picture.

events.headline: Relief at the pump, but the system stays tight
events.summary: Brent holds below $80 (~$77) as the de-escalation sticks. US commercial crude stocks fall 8.3M bbl to ~418M (≈6% below the five-year average) and UK pump prices ease (petrol 153.26p, diesel 172.47p) — relief, not resilience.

events.headline: A lopsided reopening
events.summary: The tankers conspicuously back in the strait are largely Iranian — vessels that went dark during the war switch transponders on to rush crude out under the new US licence. Some Saudi, UAE and Qatari LNG cargoes move too, but broader international traffic stays thin, far below the 100-plus ships a day seen pre-war.

events.headline: US and Iran agree a 60-day roadmap
events.summary: Talks in Switzerland produce a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal: a toll-free Strait of Hormuz, an end to Lebanon hostilities, a safe-passage communications line and a de-confliction cell. The JMIC cuts its Hormuz threat level to moderate and the US blockade ends.

events.headline: US authorises Iranian oil sales through 21 August
events.summary: The US Treasury issues OFAC General License X, authorising the production, sale and delivery of Iranian-origin crude and petroleum products through 21 August 2026 — running oil trade through the sanctions apparatus rather than excluding Iran from it.

events.headline: Explosion at Qatar's Ras Laffan gas site
events.summary: A blast at the Barzan plant in Ras Laffan during a war-halted restart kills 13 (12 of them Indian nationals) and injures 66. QatarEnergy brings the fire under control; authorities rule it a technical accident, not sabotage, with no danger to public safety.

events.headline: Iran declares Hormuz closed; the US disputes it
events.summary: Iran's military declares the Strait closed and the IRGC warns vessels away, citing Israeli strikes in Lebanon. CENTCOM disputes a physical closure — saying 55 merchant ships transited carrying ~17M barrels, a record — so the war-risk premium returns on the declaration, not a demonstrated halt.

events.headline: Switzerland talks abruptly postponed
events.summary: Planned follow-up talks in Switzerland are postponed and renewed Israeli strikes hit Lebanon, weakening the de-escalation story. Brent ticks back up from its post-deal lows as the market reprices some risk.

events.headline: US–Iran memorandum signed to end the war
events.summary: Trump and Iranian President Pezeshkian sign a memorandum of understanding to end the war; mediator Pakistan says Tehran will reopen Hormuz toll-free and the US blockade of Iranian ports will cease. Brent eases toward $80.

events.headline: Maritime threat level first reduced
events.summary: The US-led Joint Maritime Information Center reduces its Strait of Hormuz threat level after the deal, an early signal to shipping that the acute phase may be easing — though mine-clearance warnings remain.

events.headline: US lets its Russian-oil sanctions waiver lapse
events.summary: With the Iran deal raising hopes of restored supply, Washington allows its waiver on Russian-oil sanctions to expire — a sign it judged the acute supply scramble to be easing.

events.headline: Oil falls ~20% from its 2026 peak
events.summary: Crude tumbles about 20% from the 2026 highs (Brent down ~19% for May) as investors grow optimistic on a lasting ceasefire that would unlock Hormuz — even as Iran fires ballistic missiles at Kuwait and sends drones toward the strait.

events.headline: Exxon warns Brent could hit $150–160
events.summary: Exxon's Neil Chapman warns physical Brent could spike to $150–160 within weeks as inventories near record lows. The IEA describes the Hormuz disruption as the largest oil supply interruption in history, with more than a billion barrels lost.

events.headline: Trump calls off an imminent strike wave
events.summary: Trump says he has called off an imminent wave of military strikes against Iran to allow more time for negotiations; oil falls more than 10% in the days afterward as the market prices a path to de-escalation.

events.headline: Iran says Hormuz is open — then reverses within hours
events.summary: Iran's foreign minister announces the Strait is open to all shipping, but within hours the IRGC reverses course and re-declares it closed, citing ceasefire violations — Israeli strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon that killed at least 16.

events.headline: Islamabad talks fail; US declares a naval blockade
events.summary: Vice-President Vance announces the Islamabad talks between the US and Iran have failed. Trump then declares a US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, with the Navy to intercept ships that had paid transit tolls to Iran. The blockade of Iranian ports runs to late May.

events.headline: Ceasefire not honoured; ships still blocked
events.summary: An agreement to lift the blockade shows no sign of being implemented, with ships again prevented from passing. Analysts warn that even after a deal, tanker traffic will take "weeks, if not months" to normalise.

events.headline: US opens an air campaign to force Hormuz open
events.summary: The United States begins an aerial campaign against Iranian targets to reopen the Strait of Hormuz after Iran's closure — escalating the conflict from threats and missile exchanges into a sustained campaign over the waterway.

events.headline: Brent tops $100 a barrel
events.summary: Brent crude surpasses $100 a barrel as the near-total disruption of Hormuz shipping feeds through to prices. By the end of March, Brent is up about 65% (~$46) — its largest-ever monthly rise.

events.headline: Tanker Skylight struck off Oman
events.summary: The oil tanker Skylight is struck by a projectile north of Khasab, Oman, killing two Indian crew members and injuring three — an early sign of the physical danger that would empty the strait of commercial traffic.

events.headline: War begins; Hormuz traffic collapses
events.summary: The United States and Israel launch an air war on Iran (and, per reporting, kill Supreme Leader Khamenei). Within hours the IRGC warns vessels by VHF radio that no ships may pass; ship-tracking shows a roughly 70% drop in traffic as the crisis begins.
